learning_objectives: Critique GDP as a development indicator and compare alternatives
Apply the capability approach to assessing well-being
Analyze the Sustainable Development Goals as a measurement framework
readings: UN Sustainable Development Goals: Targets and Indicators (overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: Development is not just economic growth. How we define it determines what policies we pursue and whose well-being we prioritize.
key_concepts: GDP growth as development: limitations and distortions
Human Development Index: combining health, education, and income
Sen's capability approach: freedom as the purpose and means of development
Multidimensional Poverty Index: beyond income poverty
SDGs: 17 goals, 169 targets as a universal development framework
lecture_notes.demonstration: Country comparison exercise: ranking 10 countries by GDP, HDI, and MPI to reveal different development pictures
setup_requirements: UNDP HDI and MPI data tables

review.value: Sen's framework defining development as expanding people's real freedoms and capabilities (what they can do and be), not just increasing incomes
A measure that captures deprivations across health, education, and living standards simultaneously, revealing poverty that income measures alone miss

class_schedule.topic: Theories of Development: From Modernization to Post-Development
learning_objectives: Compare modernization, dependency, world-systems, and post-development theories
Analyze how development theories shape policy prescriptions
Evaluate the relevance of each theory for contemporary development challenges

lecture_notes.introduction: Every development policy rests on a theory about why some societies are poor and what will make them richer. These theories are deeply contested.
key_concepts: Modernization theory: Rostow's stages of growth, culture change
Dependency theory: structural exploitation, unequal exchange (Frank, Cardoso)
World-systems theory: core, periphery, semi-periphery (Wallerstein)
Neoliberal development: Washington Consensus and its aftermath
Post-development: rejecting Western development as a discourse of power (Escobar)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Theory-policy mapping: connecting each theory to specific development policies and outcomes

review.value: The argument that underdevelopment is not a stage but a condition created by exploitative relationships between rich core countries and poor peripheral countries
Escobar's critique that 'development' itself is a Western discourse that creates the categories of 'developed' and 'underdeveloped' to justify interventions that serve Western interests

class_schedule.topic: The Aid Industry: Effectiveness, Reform, and Alternatives
learning_objectives: Analyze the architecture of the international aid system
Evaluate evidence on aid effectiveness from RCTs and macro studies
Assess alternatives: South-South cooperation, remittances, domestic resource mobilization
readings: Riddell, Does Foreign Aid Really Work? Ch. 1-3 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Hundreds of billions of dollars flow as development aid each year. Does it work? The answer depends on what kind of aid, delivered how, and measured by what standard.
key_concepts: ODA architecture: bilateral, multilateral, NGO channels
Paris Declaration principles: ownership, alignment, harmonization, results, accountability
RCTs and evidence-based development: the Banerjee-Duflo revolution
Aid critiques: Easterly's planners vs. searchers, Moyo's Dead Aid
Alternatives: remittances, FDI, domestic taxation, South-South cooperation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating a real development project using the Paris Declaration criteria

review.value: Five principles for effective aid: country ownership, alignment with local priorities, harmonization among donors, managing for results, and mutual accountability
Experimental method that randomly assigns interventions to measure causal impact, pioneered by Banerjee, Duflo, and Kremer (Nobel 2019) for evidence-based policy

class_schedule.topic: Gender, Health, and Education in Development
learning_objectives: Analyze gender as a central dimension of development
Evaluate global health interventions and their development impacts
Assess the role of education in breaking poverty cycles

lecture_notes.introduction: Gender equality, health, and education are not just development outcomes but drivers of development. Investing in them generates cascading benefits.
key_concepts: Gender and development: from Women in Development to Gender and Development
Maternal and child health as development indicators
Global health governance: WHO, GAVI, Global Fund
Education and human capital: returns to schooling in developing countries
Conditional cash transfers: Bolsa Familia, PROGRESA as development innovations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data analysis: correlating female education rates with child mortality, GDP growth, and fertility rates across countries
setup_requirements: World Bank gender and development data

review.value: The framework that analyzes how gender relations (not just women's issues) shape development processes and outcomes, advocating for structural transformation of power relations
Programs that provide cash payments to poor families conditional on behaviors like school attendance and health checkups, pioneered in Latin America with strong evidence of effectiveness

class_schedule.topic: Governance, Corruption, and Institutions in Development
learning_objectives: Analyze the role of governance quality in development outcomes
Evaluate strategies for combating corruption in developing countries
Assess institutional perspectives on development: why institutions matter
readings: Acemoglu & Robinson, Why Nations Fail Ch. 1-2 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Institutions -- the rules of the game -- increasingly dominate explanations for why some countries are rich and others poor. Getting institutions right is the development challenge.
key_concepts: Institutions: extractive vs. inclusive (Acemoglu and Robinson)
Good governance: rule of law, accountability, transparency, participation
Corruption: causes, consequences, and measurement
Anti-corruption strategies: transparency, accountability, technology
The sequencing debate: governance first or growth first?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case comparison: Botswana vs. Sierra Leone -- how institutions explain divergent development

review.value: Acemoglu and Robinson's framework: extractive institutions concentrate power and wealth (hindering development), while inclusive institutions spread opportunity and power (promoting development)
The set of institutional qualities -- rule of law, accountability, transparency, participation, responsiveness -- that enable effective and equitable development

class_schedule.topic: Climate Change, Sustainability, and the Future of Development
learning_objectives: Analyze the tension between economic growth and environmental sustainability
Evaluate climate finance and adaptation as development priorities
Assess the concept of sustainable development and its policy implications
readings: Sachs, The Age of Sustainable Development Ch. 1 (excerpt)
IPCC, Climate Change and Land (Summary for Policymakers)

lecture_notes.introduction: Development in the 21st century must be sustainable. Climate change threatens to reverse decades of development gains while creating new patterns of vulnerability.
key_concepts: Sustainable development: Brundtland definition and evolution
Climate vulnerability and development: who is most affected
Climate finance: the $100 billion pledge and beyond
Green growth vs. degrowth debates
Just transition in developing countries: fossil fuel dependence and alternatives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy design exercise: creating a climate adaptation plan for a vulnerable developing country

review.value: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs (Brundtland Commission, 1987)
Financial flows from developed to developing countries for climate mitigation and adaptation, a key equity issue given that those most affected contributed least to emissions

questions.question: Explain Amartya Sen's capability approach to development. How does it differ from measuring development by GDP per capita, and why does this difference matter for policy?
questions.answer: Sen's capability approach defines development as expanding people's real freedoms -- their capabilities to achieve valued functionings like being healthy, educated, and politically active. It differs from GDP-based measurement because a country can have high GDP while many citizens lack basic capabilities (e.g., Saudi Arabia has high GDP but restricted women's freedoms). This matters for policy because a GDP focus leads to growth-maximizing policies that may increase inequality, while a capability focus prioritizes investments in health, education, and political freedom that expand what people can actually do and be, even if they do not maximize aggregate GDP growth.

questions.question: What are the five Paris Declaration principles for aid effectiveness? Why has 'country ownership' been particularly difficult to achieve in practice?
questions.answer: The five Paris Declaration principles are: (1) Ownership -- developing countries set their own development priorities; (2) Alignment -- donors align with those priorities; (3) Harmonization -- donors coordinate to reduce duplication; (4) Managing for Results -- focus on measurable outcomes; (5) Mutual Accountability -- both donors and recipients are accountable. Country ownership is difficult because: donors have their own domestic political pressures and strategic interests that shape aid allocation; conditionality attached to loans undermines recipient autonomy; capacity constraints in developing countries limit their ability to set and manage priorities; and the power asymmetry between donors and recipients makes genuine ownership aspirational rather than actual.

rubric.key: Explains multiple reasons ownership is difficult
Analysis shows understanding of aid dynamics

questions.question: Explain Acemoglu and Robinson's distinction between extractive and inclusive institutions. Provide one historical example showing how extractive institutions hindered development.
questions.answer: Extractive institutions concentrate political power and economic rewards in the hands of a narrow elite, creating incentives for the elite to exploit the majority rather than invest in broad-based growth. Inclusive institutions distribute power broadly through pluralistic political systems and protect property rights and economic opportunity for all. Example: colonial institutions in Congo under Belgian rule were highly extractive, designed to extract rubber, minerals, and labor for European benefit while investing nothing in local human capital or infrastructure. After independence, these extractive institutional patterns persisted, with new elites (Mobutu) replacing colonial ones in exploiting resources, contributing to Congo's continued underdevelopment despite vast natural wealth.

rubric.key: Correctly defines both types of institutions
Shows how extractive institutions hindered development

questions.question: What is the tension between economic development and environmental sustainability? Describe one approach that attempts to resolve this tension.
questions.answer: The tension arises because traditional economic development has relied on fossil fuels, resource extraction, and industrialization that generate greenhouse gases, pollution, and environmental degradation. Developing countries argue they need to grow now and should not bear the cost of a problem created by already-industrialized nations. One approach that attempts to resolve this is 'green growth' -- the strategy of pursuing economic growth through clean energy, sustainable agriculture, and circular economy principles, arguing that environmental sustainability and economic growth are compatible. Examples include Costa Rica's investment in renewable energy and ecotourism, and China's massive investment in solar panel manufacturing. Critics counter that truly sustainable development may require abandoning growth-maximization altogether (degrowth).

rubric.key: Describes a resolution approach with specifics

major_assignments.title: Development Project Evaluation
major_assignments.description: Select a real international development project or program and conduct a comprehensive evaluation using development theory, evidence standards, and the Paris Declaration framework. Produce both an academic evaluation and a practitioner-oriented recommendations report.
objectives: Apply development theories to evaluate a real intervention
Assess project effectiveness using evidence-based criteria
Analyze the political economy of development assistance
deliverables: Theory of change diagram for the project

criteria: Appropriate development theory applied to the case
Project logic and assumptions critically examined
Power relationships within the project examined
Theory-informed evaluation of a real development project
Rigorous evidence assessment with honest findings
Political economy analysis of aid dynamics

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on development concepts, theories, and case studies

participation.description: Engagement in discussions, data exercises, and policy simulations

midterm_examination.description: Exam on development theories, measurement, and aid effectiveness

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam integrating all development topics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. More than 3 unexcused absences reduce participation grade to zero.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers penalized 5% per day. Theory of change diagrams cannot be submitted late.
course_policies.collaboration: Data analysis collaboration is encouraged. All written evaluation must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citation. Fabricating evaluation data results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to World Bank, UNDP, and OECD development databases; statistical software; and course LMS.
